title: Multi-Tenancy: Shared DB, Schema per Tenant
description: Per-tenant schema within shared DB; better isolation than row-level; manageable for moderate tenant counts.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Schema-per-tenant requires a shared database instance with enough capacity to handle multiple schemas. Min ($100): Single RDS/Cloud SQL instance (db.t3.small/db-g1-small) supporting 10-50 tenant schemas with low-moderate traffic (~5-10GB storage, 2vCPU, 4GB RAM). Max ($5000): Production-grade HA database cluster (db.m5.2xlarge or equivalent) with read replicas, automated backups, point-in-time recovery, supporting 200-500+ tenant schemas with high traffic, 500GB+ storage, connection pooling infrastructure, and monitoring tools. Costs scale with: number of schemas (connection overhead), storage per tenant, query complexity, backup retention, and need for schema migration tooling across multiple schemas.
provenance.adoptionCost: High-medium complexity implementation. Requires: (1) Schema provisioning automation (create/drop schemas per tenant), (2) Tenant-aware connection routing/middleware to direct queries to correct schema, (3) Migration framework that can run across all tenant schemas atomically, (4) Backup/restore strategy per schema, (5) Monitoring for per-tenant metrics and schema-level performance, (6) Testing isolation guarantees, (7) Data residency/compliance considerations per tenant. Development time: 3-5 weeks for experienced team (schema management layer, migration tooling, tenant onboarding/offboarding automation). Learning curve moderate - requires understanding of database schema management, connection pooling, and multi-tenant routing patterns. More complex than shared-table (row-level) approach but simpler than database-per-tenant.
provenance.licenseCost: Pattern uses standard database features (schemas/namespaces) available in open-source databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL). No proprietary licensing required - schema isolation is native RDBMS functionality. Commercial database editions (Oracle, SQL Server Enterprise) could cost $15,000-$50,000+ but open-source alternatives provide equivalent capability. Any schema migration tools (Flyway, Liquibase) or tenant management frameworks are typically open-source or have free tiers sufficient for this pattern.

provides.reasoning: This pattern's core purpose is to enable multi-tenancy through schema-level isolation in a shared database, making this capability essential and appropriate.

Each tenant has its own dedicated database schema, providing schema-level isolation which is stronger than row-level but weaker than database-per-tenant. This separation limits the blast radius of issues and provides clearer security boundaries between tenants.
Schema-per-tenant isolation requires identifying the tenant at connection time to route queries to the correct schema — tenant identification is a core operational requirement.

requires.reasoning: Essential requirement since each tenant has its own schema, and managing schema migrations across potentially hundreds of tenant schemas manually would be impractical and error-prone.
This pattern requires the ability to provision new schemas when tenants are onboarded, including creating the schema structure and necessary database objects.
This pattern specifically requires a relational database that supports schema namespacing (PostgreSQL schemas, MySQL databases, etc.).
Managing connections across multiple schemas in a shared database requires careful connection management to ensure efficient resource utilization and prevent connection exhaustion.
The system must determine which schema to use for each request by identifying the tenant context, ensuring queries are routed to the correct tenant's schema.

supports_nfr.reason: Pattern explicitly provides schema-per-tenant isolation model with database-enforced boundaries between tenant schemas.
Pattern is GDPR-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is CCPA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is HIPAA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling and provider BAAs, not this pattern.
Pattern is SOX-agnostic; compliance depends on audit controls in the application layer, not this pattern.

requires_nfr.reason: Shared database infrastructure with schema-per-tenant can achieve up to 99.99% availability with proper redundancy.
Relational database with schema-level isolation introduces minimum 5ms p95 latency for tenant context resolution and query routing.
Schema-based tenant context resolution and connection pooling introduce minimum 20ms p99 latency tail for cross-schema operations.
Schema-level granularity enables selective tenant restoration from backups within 5 minutes minimum recovery time.
Schema-per-tenant supports per-schema continuous backup strategies with 1-minute minimum recovery point objective.
Shared database with schema isolation can handle up to 3.6B operations per hour before connection pool and schema routing become bottlenecks.
Schema-per-tenant shares database connection pool and resources; at >50K req/s, database-per-tenant or sharding provides better scalability.
Schema-per-tenant write throughput is bounded by shared database write capacity and schema-level locking to approximately 50K QPS.
Schema-per-tenant enables per-tenant audit logging with schema-scoped triggers and audit tables, but does not mandate it.
Schema-per-tenant provides adequate isolation for PII with database-enforced schema boundaries regardless of data content.
Schema isolation supports GDPR right-to-erasure and data portability requirements through per-tenant schema operations.
Schema-per-tenant provides HIPAA-compliant isolation with separate schemas for PHI and database-level access controls.
Schema boundaries enable CCPA compliance with isolated tenant data for deletion and export without cross-tenant leakage.
Schema-per-tenant supports SOX compliance with schema-level audit trails and data segregation for financial records.
Schema isolation within same database provides read-your-writes consistency through connection affinity and transaction guarantees.
Shared database provides strict durability guarantees through ACID transactions and WAL regardless of schema isolation model.

supports_constraints.reason: Schema-per-tenant pattern is database-agnostic and works on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure with relational database support.
This is a database architecture pattern that can be implemented in any language with database connectivity capabilities.
This is a backend database architecture pattern that can be used by any platform type requiring multi-tenant data isolation.
Schema-per-tenant database pattern works with both synchronous and asynchronous messaging architectures for tenant operations.
Schema-per-tenant pattern can store data for AI inference workloads or traditional applications with equivalent tenant isolation.
Schema-per-tenant supports both real-time transactional and batch processing workloads with per-tenant schema scoping.
Schema-per-tenant can participate in distributed transactions or operate with local transactions within single database instance.
Schema-per-tenant pattern works with or without real-time streaming data ingestion into tenant-scoped schemas.
Schema-per-tenant can store vector embeddings in tenant schemas if database supports vector extensions like pgvector.
Schema-per-tenant pattern can use JSON columns or document-oriented features within relational schemas for semi-structured data.
Schema-per-tenant relational pattern can implement key-value semantics using simple two-column tables within tenant schemas.
Schema-per-tenant can model graph relationships using foreign keys or work with graph extensions in tenant-scoped schemas.
Schema-per-tenant can store time-series data in tenant schemas using timestamp-indexed tables or time-series extensions.
Schema-per-tenant pattern is designed for transactional workloads where tenants require isolated schemas for OLTP operations.
Schema-per-tenant primarily serves OLTP but can support analytical queries with appropriate indexing and query optimization per schema.

requires_constraints.reason: Pattern explicitly requires multi-tenancy support as it is designed specifically for multi-tenant database isolation.

schema_naming_strategy.description: How to name tenant schemas. tenant_id uses numeric IDs (schema_123), tenant_name uses slugified names (schema_acme_corp), custom_prefix allows domain-specific naming.

migration_strategy.description: How to handle schema migrations. apply_to_all runs migrations on all tenant schemas immediately, lazy_migration updates schemas on first tenant access, versioned_schemas allows different tenants to run different schema versions.

tenant_provisioning.description: When to create tenant schemas. on_demand creates schemas during tenant signup, pre_provisioned creates a pool of ready schemas, template_clone duplicates a template schema for faster provisioning.

max_tenants.description: Maximum number of tenant schemas to support. This pattern works best with 10-500 tenants; beyond that, consider shard-per-tenant or pooled schemas.

connection_pooling.description: Connection pool strategy. per_schema maintains separate pools per tenant for isolation, shared_pool uses one pool with schema switching for efficiency, dynamic adjusts pool sizes based on tenant activity.

warn_nfr.reason: Schema-per-tenant adds schema routing and connection management overhead; at <10 req/s, simpler row-level isolation may be more appropriate.
warn_nfr.message: tenancy-schema-per-tenant: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<10). Schema-level isolation overhead may outweigh benefits at this low throughput; consider row-level multi-tenancy instead.
